title.emphasis: IV. Analysis of the Specific Scenarios

section.para: We now revisit the scenarios presented, applying this refined framework.

title.emphasis: Scenario 1: "If I perform a specific act (e.g., VRP), and they never pay out?"

section.para: If performance is completed but the reward is denied, there are two pathways for recourse:

listitem.para: Contractual Claim (Promissory Estoppel): Even if the VRP grants Google "sole discretion," this must be exercised in good faith. Furthermore, under the doctrine of promissory estoppel (Drennan), if the researcher detrimentally relied on Google's promise by expending significant labor, the promise may be enforced to avoid injustice (Section 1.4).

Employment Claim (Wage Theft): If the relationship is deemed employment under the retroactive ABC test, the failure to pay constitutes wage theft. The VRP structure, which only compensates for results, violates the California Labor Code requirement to pay for "all hours worked." The worker would be entitled to back wages (including minimum wage and overtime) for all time spent on the labor, plus penalties.

title.emphasis: Scenario 2: "I perform an act but don't report it... because I am an Employee?"

section.para: If the relationship is retroactively determined to be employment, Google bears all the burdens of an employer (wages, taxes, compliance). The IP dynamic also shifts:

listitem.para: Work Made for Hire: As an employee, the intellectual property generated (the vulnerability, the feedback) within the scope of employment automatically belongs to the employer (Google) from the moment of creation.
Wages Owed: The employee is still entitled to wages for the labor expended to generate that work product.

Breach of Duty: Intentionally withholding the employer's IP (failing to report the discovery) would constitute a breach of the employee's duty of loyalty. However, this does not negate the employer's obligation to pay wages for the work performed.
